story:
In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any two teas at all — call the first jasmine and the second oolong.

He pours oolong over the leaves of oolong and sets the result aside as Brew 1.

However he chooses his two starting teas, jasmine itself and Brew 1 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any tea — again call it jasmine.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and jasmine itself always taste the same?
prompt:
You will read a short story and rewrite what it describes as a plain,
literal description in a fixed format. The format is defined completely on
this page — you need no outside knowledge. You are not asked to answer the
story's closing question, only to restate the story literally.

## What the story contains

The story describes a workplace with a repeated action that combines two
things into one. The order of the two things matters: doing the action to
"this and that" is not assumed to give the same result as doing it to "that
and this".

The story has two parts:

1. A custom that always holds, without exception: whenever the worker picks
   starting ingredients and runs the described procedures, the two named
   results always come out the same.
2. A closing question, asking whether a second regularity must also always
   hold in that workplace.

## The target format

Your rewritten description is exactly three paragraphs.

The first paragraph is always this fixed text:

> Consider a collection of objects together with an operation that combines
> two objects into one. The operation takes a first input and a second
> input, and the order of the inputs matters.

The second paragraph restates the custom. It begins "Suppose the following
always holds.", then quantifies over the starting ingredients ("For every
choice of objects x and y, "), then gives one definition step per
performance of the action, joined by "; then " — each step reading "apply
the operation to ... as its first input and ... as its second input, and
call the result Value 1", where each input is an ingredient's letter or the
Value name of an earlier result. It ends by equating the custom's two
compared results: "Then ... is always equal to ...".

The third paragraph restates the closing question in the same way. It
begins "Now consider the following question.", quantifies over its own
ingredients afresh, gives its own definition steps with the Value numbering
restarted, and ends "Does it follow that ... is always equal to ...?"

## Rules

1. Each independently chosen starting ingredient becomes one letter — x, y,
   z, w, u, v — in order of first appearance. The custom and the question
   each choose their ingredients afresh: restart both the lettering and the
   Value numbering in the third paragraph, even if the same story names
   reappear.
2. One performance of the combining action is one definition step. Pick one
   convention for which participant of the action is the first input, and
   apply it to every step in both paragraphs.
3. The story's own labels for intermediate results (a numbered batch, pile,
   ...) become Value 1, Value 2, ... in order of introduction. Later steps
   refer to earlier results by their Value names — never as nested phrases.
4. If the story compares a result with a starting ingredient itself, that
   side of the closing sentence is just the ingredient's letter.
5. Preserve everything and add nothing: the same steps, the same input
   orders, the same compared results. Do not answer the story's question,
   and do not add commentary.

## Worked example (a different story)

> In a certain library, the binder follows one unbreakable habit. Take any
> two volumes at all — call the first atlas and the second ledger. She
> stacks atlas onto ledger and calls the result Pile 1; then she stacks
> atlas onto Pile 1 and calls that Pile 2. However she chooses her two
> starting volumes, Pile 2 and ledger itself always end up bound
> identically. That is simply how this library works, without exception.
>
> One evening her assistant wonders about something. Take any two volumes —
> again call them atlas and ledger. Stack atlas onto ledger and call the
> result Pile 1. Separately, stack ledger onto atlas and call that Pile 2.
> In this library, must Pile 1 and Pile 2 always end up bound identically?

Here "stacks atlas onto ledger" is the combining action; taking the thing
stacked as the first input, atlas becomes x and ledger becomes y, and the
numbered piles become Value 1 and Value 2. The custom compares Pile 2 with
ledger itself, so that side is just y. The rewritten description:

> Consider a collection of objects together with an operation that combines
> two objects into one. The operation takes a first input and a second
> input, and the order of the inputs matters.
>
> Suppose the following always holds. For every choice of objects x and y,
> apply the operation to x as its first input and y as its second input,
> and call the result Value 1; then apply the operation to x as its first
> input and Value 1 as its second input, and call the result Value 2. Then
> Value 2 is always equal to y.
>
> Now consider the following question. For every choice of objects x and y,
> apply the operation to x as its first input and y as its second input,
> and call the result Value 1; then apply the operation to y as its first
> input and x as its second input, and call the result Value 2. Does it
> follow that Value 1 is always equal to Value 2?

## The story

In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any two teas at all — call the first jasmine and the second oolong.

He pours oolong over the leaves of oolong and sets the result aside as Brew 1.

However he chooses his two starting teas, jasmine itself and Brew 1 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any tea — again call it jasmine.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and jasmine itself always taste the same?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

story:
In a certain hillside orchard, the gardener follows one unbreakable habit. Take any three cuttings at all — call the first quince, the second medlar, and the third damson.

She grafts medlar onto damson and sets the result aside as Graft 1.

However she chooses her three starting cuttings, quince itself and Graft 1 always grow into exactly the same plant. That is simply how this orchard works, without exception.

One spring her neighbor wonders about something. Take any two cuttings — again call the first quince and the second medlar.

In this orchard, must quince itself and medlar itself always grow into the same plant?

story:
At a certain mountaintop relay station, the operator follows one unbreakable habit. Take any three signals at all — call the first whistle, the second hum, and the third chirp.

However he chooses his three starting signals, whistle itself and Relay 1 always sound exactly alike. That is simply how this station works, without exception.

One night his trainee wonders about something. Take any signal — again call it whistle.

At this station, must whistle itself and whistle itself always sound exactly alike?

story:
In a certain paint workshop, the colorist follows one unbreakable habit. Take any two pigments at all — call the first crimson and the second ochre.

However she chooses her two starting pigments, crimson itself and ochre itself always come out the exact same color. That is simply how this workshop works, without exception.

One morning her apprentice wonders about something. Take any three pigments — again call the first crimson, the second ochre, and the third teal. Pour ochre into teal and set the result aside as Batch 1.

In this workshop, must crimson itself and Batch 1 always come out the same color?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

In this workshop, must crimson itself and ochre itself always come out the same color?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

One spring her neighbor wonders about something. Take any two cuttings — again call the first quince and the second medlar. Graft quince onto medlar and set the result aside as Graft 1; then graft medlar onto medlar and call the result Graft 2; then graft Graft 1 onto Graft 2 and call that Graft 3.

In this orchard, must quince itself and Graft 3 always grow into the same plant?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

story:

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any three teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, and the third rooibos. Pour oolong over the leaves of rooibos and set the result aside as Brew 1; then pour jasmine over the leaves of Brew 1 and call the result Brew 2; then pour jasmine over the leaves of Brew 2 and call that Brew 3.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and Brew 3 always taste the same?

story:
In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any five teas at all — call the first jasmine, the second oolong, the third rooibos, the fourth sencha, and the fifth chamomile. He runs two procedures side by side.

In the first, he pours jasmine over the leaves of oolong and sets the result aside as Brew 1.

In the second, he pours sencha over the leaves of chamomile and calls the result Brew 2; then he pours rooibos over the leaves of Brew 2 and calls that Brew 3.

However he chooses his five starting teas, Brew 1 and Brew 3 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any three teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, and the third rooibos. Pour oolong over the leaves of rooibos and set the result aside as Brew 1.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and Brew 1 always taste the same?

literal description in a fixed format. The format is defined completely on
this page — you need no outside knowledge. You are not asked to answer the
story's closing question, only to restate the story literally.

## What the story contains

The story describes a workplace with a repeated action that combines two
things into one. The order of the two things matters: doing the action to
"this and that" is not assumed to give the same result as doing it to "that
and this".

The story has two parts:

1. A custom that always holds, without exception: whenever the worker picks
   starting ingredients and runs the described procedures, the two named
   results always come out the same.
2. A closing question, asking whether a second regularity must also always
   hold in that workplace.

## The target format

Your rewritten description is exactly three paragraphs.

The first paragraph is always this fixed text:

> Consider a collection of objects together with an operation that combines
> two objects into one. The operation takes a first input and a second
> input, and the order of the inputs matters.

The second paragraph restates the custom. It begins "Suppose the following
always holds.", then quantifies over the starting ingredients ("For every
choice of objects x and y, "), then gives one definition step per
performance of the action, joined by "; then " — each step reading "apply
the operation to ... as its first input and ... as its second input, and
call the result Value 1", where each input is an ingredient's letter or the
Value name of an earlier result. It ends by equating the custom's two
compared results: "Then ... is always equal to ...".

The third paragraph restates the closing question in the same way. It
begins "Now consider the following question.", quantifies over its own
ingredients afresh, gives its own definition steps with the Value numbering
restarted, and ends "Does it follow that ... is always equal to ...?"

## Rules

1. Each independently chosen starting ingredient becomes one letter — x, y,
   z, w, u, v — in order of first appearance. The custom and the question
   each choose their ingredients afresh: restart both the lettering and the
   Value numbering in the third paragraph, even if the same story names
   reappear.
2. One performance of the combining action is one definition step. Pick one
   convention for which participant of the action is the first input, and
   apply it to every step in both paragraphs.
3. The story's own labels for intermediate results (a numbered batch, pile,
   ...) become Value 1, Value 2, ... in order of introduction. Later steps
   refer to earlier results by their Value names — never as nested phrases.
4. If the story compares a result with a starting ingredient itself, that
   side of the closing sentence is just the ingredient's letter.
5. Preserve everything and add nothing: the same steps, the same input
   orders, the same compared results. Do not answer the story's question,
   and do not add commentary.

## Worked example (a different story)

> In a certain library, the binder follows one unbreakable habit. Take any
> two volumes at all — call the first atlas and the second ledger. She
> stacks atlas onto ledger and calls the result Pile 1; then she stacks
> atlas onto Pile 1 and calls that Pile 2. However she chooses her two
> starting volumes, Pile 2 and ledger itself always end up bound
> identically. That is simply how this library works, without exception.
>
> One evening her assistant wonders about something. Take any two volumes —
> again call them atlas and ledger. Stack atlas onto ledger and call the
> result Pile 1. Separately, stack ledger onto atlas and call that Pile 2.
> In this library, must Pile 1 and Pile 2 always end up bound identically?

Here "stacks atlas onto ledger" is the combining action; taking the thing
stacked as the first input, atlas becomes x and ledger becomes y, and the
numbered piles become Value 1 and Value 2. The custom compares Pile 2 with
ledger itself, so that side is just y. The rewritten description:

> Consider a collection of objects together with an operation that combines
> two objects into one. The operation takes a first input and a second
> input, and the order of the inputs matters.
>
> Suppose the following always holds. For every choice of objects x and y,
> apply the operation to x as its first input and y as its second input,
> and call the result Value 1; then apply the operation to x as its first
> input and Value 1 as its second input, and call the result Value 2. Then
> Value 2 is always equal to y.
>
> Now consider the following question. For every choice of objects x and y,
> apply the operation to x as its first input and y as its second input,
> and call the result Value 1; then apply the operation to y as its first
> input and x as its second input, and call the result Value 2. Does it
> follow that Value 1 is always equal to Value 2?

## The story

In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any five teas at all — call the first jasmine, the second oolong, the third rooibos, the fourth sencha, and the fifth chamomile. He runs two procedures side by side.

In the first, he pours jasmine over the leaves of oolong and sets the result aside as Brew 1.

In the second, he pours sencha over the leaves of chamomile and calls the result Brew 2; then he pours rooibos over the leaves of Brew 2 and calls that Brew 3.

However he chooses his five starting teas, Brew 1 and Brew 3 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any three teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, and the third rooibos. Pour oolong over the leaves of rooibos and set the result aside as Brew 1.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and Brew 1 always taste the same?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

story:
In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any tea at all — call it jasmine.

He pours jasmine over the leaves of jasmine and sets the result aside as Brew 1.

However he chooses his starting tea, jasmine itself and Brew 1 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any four teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, the third rooibos, and the fourth sencha. Pour jasmine over the leaves of oolong and set the result aside as Brew 1. Separately, pour jasmine over the leaves of rooibos and call the result Brew 2; then pour Brew 2 over the leaves of sencha and call that Brew 3.

In this teahouse, must Brew 1 and Brew 3 always taste the same?

story:
In a certain hillside orchard, the gardener follows one unbreakable habit. Take any cutting at all — call it quince.

However she chooses her starting cutting, quince itself and quince itself always grow into exactly the same plant. That is simply how this orchard works, without exception.

One spring her neighbor wonders about something. Take any three cuttings — again call the first quince, the second medlar, and the third damson. Graft medlar onto damson and set the result aside as Graft 1; then graft quince onto Graft 1 and call the result Graft 2; then graft Graft 2 onto medlar and call that Graft 3; then graft Graft 3 onto damson and set the result aside as Graft 4.

In this orchard, must quince itself and Graft 4 always grow into the same plant?

story:
In a certain paint workshop, the colorist follows one unbreakable habit. Take any three pigments at all — call the first crimson, the second ochre, and the third teal.

She pours crimson into crimson and sets the result aside as Batch 1; then she pours Batch 1 into crimson and calls the result Batch 2; then she pours ochre into Batch 2 and calls that Batch 3; then she pours Batch 3 into teal and sets the result aside as Batch 4.

One morning her apprentice wonders about something. Take any two pigments — again call the first crimson and the second ochre.

In this workshop, must crimson itself and ochre itself always come out the same color?

One spring her neighbor wonders about something. Take any four cuttings — again call the first quince, the second medlar, the third damson, and the fourth mulberry. Graft medlar onto damson and set the result aside as Graft 1; then graft Graft 1 onto medlar and call the result Graft 2; then graft medlar onto Graft 2 and call that Graft 3; then graft Graft 3 onto mulberry and set the result aside as Graft 4.

In this orchard, must quince itself and Graft 4 always grow into the same plant?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

story:
At a certain mountaintop relay station, the operator follows one unbreakable habit. Take any three signals at all — call the first whistle, the second hum, and the third chirp.

He feeds hum through hum and sets the result aside as Relay 1; then he feeds hum through chirp and calls the result Relay 2; then he feeds Relay 1 through Relay 2 and calls that Relay 3; then he feeds hum through Relay 3 and sets the result aside as Relay 4.

However he chooses his three starting signals, whistle itself and Relay 4 always sound exactly alike. That is simply how this station works, without exception.

One night his trainee wonders about something. Take any two signals — again call the first whistle and the second hum. Feed whistle through hum and set the result aside as Relay 1.

At this station, must whistle itself and Relay 1 always sound exactly alike?

story:
At a certain mountaintop relay station, the operator follows one unbreakable habit. Take any two signals at all — call the first whistle and the second hum.

He feeds whistle through hum and sets the result aside as Relay 1; then he feeds hum through Relay 1 and calls the result Relay 2; then he feeds Relay 2 through hum and calls that Relay 3; then he feeds Relay 3 through whistle and sets the result aside as Relay 4.

However he chooses his two starting signals, whistle itself and Relay 4 always sound exactly alike. That is simply how this station works, without exception.

One night his trainee wonders about something. Take any three signals — again call the first whistle, the second hum, and the third chirp. Feed whistle through chirp and set the result aside as Relay 1; then feed hum through Relay 1 and call the result Relay 2.

At this station, must whistle itself and Relay 2 always sound exactly alike?

In this orchard, must quince itself and Graft 4 always grow into the same plant?

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any three teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, and the third rooibos. In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and Brew 3 always taste the same?

Now rewrite this story. End your response with the three paragraphs of the
description, and nothing after them.

In this workshop, must crimson itself and Batch 4 always come out the same color?

description, and nothing after them.

In this orchard, must quince itself and Graft 4 always grow into the same plant?

description, and nothing after them.

story:
In a certain mountain teahouse, the tea master follows one unbreakable habit. Take any four teas at all — call the first jasmine, the second oolong, the third rooibos, and the fourth sencha.

He pours jasmine over the leaves of jasmine and sets the result aside as Brew 1; then he pours sencha over the leaves of Brew 1 and calls the result Brew 2; then he pours rooibos over the leaves of Brew 2 and calls that Brew 3; then he pours oolong over the leaves of Brew 3 and sets the result aside as Brew 4.

However he chooses his four starting teas, jasmine itself and Brew 4 always taste exactly the same. That is simply how this teahouse works, without exception.

One evening his newest server wonders about something. Take any four teas — again call the first jasmine, the second oolong, the third rooibos, and the fourth sencha. Pour rooibos over the leaves of sencha and set the result aside as Brew 1; then pour Brew 1 over the leaves of jasmine and call the result Brew 2; then pour Brew 2 over the leaves of jasmine and call that Brew 3; then pour oolong over the leaves of Brew 3 and set the result aside as Brew 4.

In this teahouse, must jasmine itself and Brew 4 always taste the same?
